Logic & Critical Thinking: A toothpaste ad says, “Nine out of ten dentists recommend it.” What would you ask before assuming it is the best choice?
A politician says a policy worked because crime dropped after it passed. What else would you check before accepting that argument?
A phone company says its plan is better because it has the most customers. What does popularity fail to prove?
A restaurant has one bad online review after a storm delayed service. What conclusion would be too strong?
A salesperson tells you, “Only smart buyers choose this warranty.” What is the pressure tactic in that argument?
A classmate says their answer must be right because they finished first. What does speed fail to prove?
A company says its product is “premium” because it costs more. What is the weak link in that claim?
A manager says a quiet employee has no ideas. What assumption should be tested?
A social media post says, “Everyone is talking about this, so it must matter.” What is missing from that reasoning?
A friend says college is useless because one famous entrepreneur dropped out. What is the problem with that example?
A brand uses one emotional customer story instead of data. What kind of evidence is missing?
A debate opponent attacks your attitude instead of answering your point. How would you bring the discussion back to the claim?
A headline says a new mayor caused higher rent after one month in office. What timing question should you ask?
A coach says the team lost because the players “didn’t want it enough.” What evidence would make that explanation stronger?
A coworker rejects an idea because it came from another department. What reasoning mistake might that be?
A company says its workers are happy because few people quit. What other explanations could exist?
A friend says a rumor is true because it sounds believable. What should belief be based on instead?
A political ad shows an opponent looking angry but gives no policy details. What is the ad trying to make you assume?
A business says its new app is safe because it has never been hacked. What does that claim fail to prove?
A teacher says strict rules improve learning because test scores rose. What other causes should be considered?
A YouTube creator says critics are jealous, so their criticism should be ignored. What claim is being avoided?
A store says an item is “almost sold out” to push a quick purchase. What evidence would confirm the claim?
A friend says their workout plan works because they feel better after one week. What else could explain the improvement?
A company says its CEO is successful because the stock price rose. What other factors might have caused the rise?
A student says a rule is unfair because they dislike it. What is missing from that argument?
A news guest repeats the same claim loudly several times. What does repetition fail to prove?
A car dealer says the monthly payment is low, so the deal is good. What information is missing?
A coworker says a plan cannot work because it failed once before. What would make that conclusion too strong?
A friend says, “If you disagree with me, you don’t care.” What false choice is being created?
A business uses a complicated chart to sound convincing. What simple question should you ask first?
Epistemology: Two doctors recommend different treatments for the same condition. What information would help you decide which advice to trust?
A friend sends you a viral video with no source. What would you check before believing it?
A screenshot appears to show someone saying something offensive. What should you verify before judging them?
A family story has been repeated for years. What evidence would help you know whether it is accurate?
A podcast guest says they “did their own research.” What would make their conclusion trustworthy?
A professional-looking website lists no author. How much should design affect your trust?
A famous actor gives health advice outside their expertise. How should fame affect your confidence?
Two witnesses remember the same car accident differently. What could explain the difference?
A news site corrects an earlier mistake. Should that raise or lower your trust, and why?
A study summary sounds convincing, but the full study is not available. How should that affect your certainty?
A friend says, “I know what I felt.” When is a feeling useful evidence and when is it not?
A rumor spreads before any official report appears. What should happen to your confidence level?
An old belief has been accepted for centuries. What does age prove, and what does it not prove?
A website lists sources, but the sources all quote each other. What problem does that create?
A source you dislike makes a claim with strong evidence. How can you judge the claim fairly?
An expert changes their recommendation after new data appears. When should that increase your trust?
A prediction about the economy sounds certain. What would make you treat it as uncertain?
A person uses technical words you do not understand. What should you ask before trusting them?
A news clip includes a real quote but leaves out the rest of the speech. What should you check?
A personal experience strongly shaped your opinion. What other evidence should you compare it with?
A group you trust all agrees on an issue. What would keep trust from becoming blind agreement?
A teacher and a YouTuber explain the same topic differently. What would help you decide who understands it better?
A photo seems to prove a claim, but you do not know when it was taken. What should you verify?
A friend admits they were wrong after checking the facts. What does that reveal about their reliability?
A poll says most people agree. What does that prove, and what does it not prove?
A neighbor says they heard a warning from “someone connected.” What would you need before acting on it?
A documentary feels convincing because the music is powerful. What evidence should matter more than the feeling?
A product has thousands of five-star reviews. What would you check before trusting the rating?
A coach says a player has “bad character” based on one incident. What else would you need to know?
A news story relies on anonymous sources. What should you ask before trusting the report?
Ethics & Moral Reasoning: A student finds answers to tomorrow’s test online. Who is harmed if they use them?
A manager can save money by cutting hours for part-time workers. What responsibilities should guide the decision?
A company uses cheaper materials that are legal but less safe. What should matter besides profit?
A friend asks you to lie so they avoid consequences. What does loyalty require here?
A city project will help thousands but damage one small neighborhood. What should leaders weigh before approving it?
A nurse has limited time and two patients need help. What is the fairest way to decide who comes first?
A business owner discovers a pricing mistake that benefits them. What should they do before keeping the money?
A school punishes a whole class because no one admits who broke a rule. Is that fair?
A family member needs money, but helping them may hurt your own household. What responsibility comes first?
A leader hides bad news to prevent panic. When does protecting people become misleading them?
A boss gives a family member a job over a more qualified applicant. What makes that ethically risky?
A student sees a friend cheating but knows reporting them could end the friendship. What should matter most?
A company moves jobs overseas to survive. What responsibilities does it still have to current workers?
A person finds a wallet with cash and an ID. What should they consider before deciding what to do?
A teacher curves grades to help struggling students, but top students lose an advantage. Is that fair?
A city bans sleeping in public places without enough shelter beds. What moral problem does that create?
A parent tracks a teenager’s phone for safety. When does protection become a lack of trust?
A company designs addictive features to keep users scrolling. What responsibility does it have?
A friend shares private information to protect someone from harm. When is breaking confidence justified?
A coach benches a star player for breaking team rules before a championship. What should guide the decision?
A business owner underpays workers because competitors do the same. Does that excuse the choice?
A family must decide whether to keep an elderly parent at home or use professional care. What values compete?
A school uses strict discipline to keep order. When does order come at too high a cost?
A shopper buys cheap goods knowing workers may be mistreated. How much responsibility does the buyer have?
A principal tells a partial truth to keep parents calm. When is that still dishonest?
A coworker takes credit for your idea, but the project succeeds. What response is fair and wise?
A government policy helps most citizens but harms a small group. What should justice require?
A friend asks for forgiveness but has not repaired the harm. What does accountability require?
A company donates to charity while mistreating employees. How should the good and bad be weighed?
A hospital has one open appointment and two urgent patients. What is the fairest way to decide?
Strategic Thinking: Netflix invested in streaming while Blockbuster stayed focused on stores. What assumptions made one strategy stronger?
Kodak helped invent digital photography but stayed attached to film. What future risk did it underestimate?
A company cuts employee training to improve quarterly profit. What might happen over the next three years?
A store lowers prices to beat competitors, but profits shrink. What could happen if this continues for six months?
A city delays road repairs to save money this year. What future costs might grow?
A startup hires fast to impress investors. What could go wrong if the culture is not ready?
A family buys a bigger house because interest rates are low. What risks should they think through first?
A student takes an easy class to protect their GPA. What could that choice cost later?
A manager avoids a hard conversation to keep peace. What could that silence cost the team later?
A politician chooses a popular short-term policy with long-term costs. What incentives are at work?
A business depends on one major customer for most revenue. What risk should it plan for?
A brand chases every trend online. What could happen to its identity?
A leader announces a bold goal before resources are ready. What strategic risk does that create?
A family pays off a low-interest loan early while ignoring emergency savings. What tradeoff matters?
A startup copies a competitor’s feature. What could make copying a weak strategy?
A person cuts off a difficult relationship quickly. What should they consider before burning the bridge?
A team hides small problems to look successful. What happens when those problems compound?
A store sells a popular product at a loss to bring people in. What must be true for that to work?
A student picks easier goals to avoid failure. What does that strategy cost over time?
A manager delays replacing outdated software. What hidden costs can grow quietly?
A community rejects new housing to protect neighborhood character. What future tradeoff might appear?
A person makes a public promise before knowing the details. What strategic mistake could that be?
A company gives big discounts to grow fast. What happens if customers only stay for low prices?
A worker accepts higher pay at a company with high turnover. What should they think through?
A city attracts tourists but prices out local families. What second-order effects should leaders expect?
A teenager posts a harsh joke to get attention. What second-order effects could follow?
A business ignores maintenance to save money. What could that decision cost later?
A school bans phones completely. What new problems might that solve and create?
A leader wins an argument publicly but embarrasses a teammate. What problem might appear next?
A negotiation offer expires today. What should you check before letting urgency decide?
Street Lessons: A friend only calls when they need money, rides, or favors. What should you consider before calling that loyalty?
A coworker asks you to sign off on work you did not do because “everyone does it.” What should you consider before saying yes?
Someone keeps complimenting you before asking for a big favor. What possibilities should you consider?
A friend asks to borrow money but has not paid back others. What should you think through before saying yes?
A person gets angry when you set a boundary. What might that reveal about the relationship?
Someone offers you a quick way to make money but avoids details. What should you watch for?
A group pressures you to react publicly to an insult. When is silence the stronger move?
A new friend shares private gossip about others. What might they do with your business later?
A boss says, “Trust me,” but will not put the agreement in writing. What is the risk?
Someone wants your loyalty before they have shown character. What would you need to see first?
A person asks for your help but gets angry when you ask questions. What should that reaction tell you?
A friend only respects boundaries after there is a consequence. What pattern should you notice?
Someone offers access to powerful people but wants money first. What should you verify?
A neighbor wants you to choose sides before you know the full story. What risk are they creating for you?
A coworker shares secrets to make you feel included. What price might come with that access?
A person apologizes in private but disrespects you in public. Which behavior should carry more weight?
Someone pressures you to decide immediately. What might speed be hiding?
A friend celebrates your wins only when they benefit from them. What should you watch for?
Someone borrows your name to get credibility. What could happen if they act badly?
A person is generous when people are watching but careless when no one sees. What does that reveal?
A group laughs at someone who is not in the room. What should you assume they may do to you?
A person makes every disagreement about respect. What kind of situation can that create?
Someone wants forgiveness but refuses to change the behavior. What boundary protects you?
A deal sounds good because you badly need it. How can desperation weaken judgment?
A friend asks you to keep a secret that could hurt someone else. What does loyalty require?
Someone gives you a gift and later acts like you owe them. What should you notice?
A person gets embarrassed and starts making threats. What risk does pride create?
Someone wants your trust but avoids small responsibilities. What does that predict?
A bad decision would be exciting today but hard to explain tomorrow. What should that tell you?
A person treats service workers badly but treats powerful people well. What character signal is that?
Financial Judgment: You receive a $10,000 bonus. What would help you decide whether to pay debt, invest, or keep cash?
A car dealer focuses only on the monthly payment. What numbers should the buyer ask for?
A store offers zero-interest financing if you buy today. What should you check before agreeing?
A friend says a stock is guaranteed to go up. What questions should you ask before investing?
A family wants a bigger car payment because the monthly cost seems manageable. What else matters?
Someone wants to use a credit card for a vacation they cannot afford now. What is the real cost?
A worker gets a raise and immediately upgrades their lifestyle. What risk does that create?
A couple has no emergency fund but wants to invest aggressively. What tradeoff should they consider?
A person chooses the cheapest car insurance plan. What could make that choice expensive later?
A small business owner discounts heavily to get customers. What should they know about margins?
A retiree considers helping an adult child financially while carrying credit card debt. What should they protect first?
A person gets a tax refund and wants to spend it immediately. What should they check first?
A friend wants to split rent but has unstable income. What agreement should be clear first?
A worker has credit card debt but wants to invest in a risky stock. What tradeoff matters?
A family buys a home at the top of their budget. What could make the payment dangerous later?
A student chooses a college based only on the dream job afterward. What financial questions should they ask?
A small business has strong sales but weak cash flow. Why can that still be dangerous?
A person leases a luxury car to look successful. What hidden cost might they be accepting?
A retiree is offered a high-return investment by a friendly advisor. What should they verify?
A couple argues about spending because one values security and one values enjoyment. What should they clarify?
A teenager gets their first paycheck. What habit would matter more than the amount?
A person wants to lend money to a relative from their emergency fund. What risk should they protect against?
A company offers stock options instead of higher pay. What should an employee understand?
A borrower refinances debt to lower the monthly payment. What total cost should they compare?
A family buys in bulk to save money but throws food away. What is the real savings?
A person follows a finance influencer’s advice without knowing their incentives. What should they ask?
A business owner expands before the first location is stable. What financial risk appears?
A worker chooses overtime for extra money but burns out. What cost is not on the paycheck?
A person wants to retire early. What assumptions should be tested before trusting the plan?
A friend says rent is wasted money and buying is always better. What details could change that?
Leadership & Influence: A team member keeps missing deadlines but is well-liked. How should a leader handle it without damaging trust?
A manager takes credit for a team’s work in front of executives. What does that do to influence?
A coach benches the best player for skipping practice. What message does that send to the team?
A principal announces a new school rule without explaining why. What could that do to trust?
A leader promises change but never follows up. What happens to credibility over time?
An employee makes a serious mistake and admits it quickly. How should a leader respond?
A team is confused because priorities keep changing. What should the leader clarify first?
A popular employee breaks a rule that others follow. What should accountability look like?
A leader wants buy-in for a hard decision. What should they explain before asking people to commit?
A supervisor avoids feedback because they want to be liked. What does that cost the team?
A new leader inherits a team that distrusts management. What first move could rebuild credibility?
A leader gives vague praise but no direction. What does the team still need?
A manager avoids holding a top performer accountable. What message does that send to everyone else?
A quiet employee has thoughtful ideas one-on-one but rarely speaks in meetings. How should a leader draw them out?
A leader changes priorities after every customer complaint. What does that do to the team?
A supervisor gives feedback only when angry. How does timing affect influence?
A team trusts a leader personally but doubts their competence. What should the leader address first?
A leader asks for honesty but punishes bad news. What culture will develop?
A manager wants people to stay late but leaves early. What influence problem is created?
A respected employee spreads negativity. How should a leader respond without overreacting?
A new policy is unpopular but necessary. What should a leader explain clearly?
A leader listens only to the loudest person in the room. What damage can that cause?
A coach praises effort but only rewards results. What behavior will players learn?
A manager inherits a team with low morale. What first action would show seriousness?
A leader must choose between speed and team understanding. When is slowing down smarter?
A supervisor wants loyalty but has not shown fairness. What should they build first?
A leader admits a mistake publicly. How can that strengthen rather than weaken authority?
A team member challenges a decision respectfully. What response protects trust?
A manager gives a difficult assignment without explaining why it matters. What is missing?
A leader relies on charisma but avoids systems. What happens when they are absent?
Media & Information Literacy: Two news outlets cover the same protest but show different footage. What should you ask before deciding what happened?
A headline says a celebrity destroyed a company, but the article is mostly opinion. What should you separate?
A viral post uses one shocking statistic with no source. What should you check before sharing it?
A video clip starts after an argument already began. What context might be missing?
An influencer reviews a product but may be paid by the brand. What should viewers know?
A news story quotes only people who agree. What would make the coverage stronger?
A YouTube chart looks dramatic because the scale starts at 90. What is the visual trying to do?
A politician’s speech is summarized by a hostile account. What should you watch or read before reacting?
On X, a trend says everyone is angry about an issue. What might the algorithm be amplifying?
A documentary tells a powerful story but leaves out opposing evidence. What should you look for next?
A headline uses the word “slams” instead of “criticizes.” How does that shape the reader’s reaction?
A viral image is real but from a different year. What mistake could viewers make?
A creator says the media will not tell you this, then sells a course. What incentive should you notice?
Two articles use the same facts but different photos. How can images change the story?
A statistic is shared without saying who was surveyed. What context is missing?
A news clip shows a crowd booing but not what happened before. What should you ask?
A post says a problem is everywhere because you saw five examples. What might the feed be doing?
A headline asks an alarming question but gives little evidence. What is the tactic?
A commentator mixes facts with jokes and insults. How can entertainment weaken judgment?
A chart compares numbers without showing population size. What could be misleading?
A breaking-news account posts before facts are confirmed. What should happen to your confidence?
A documentary uses sad music during one side’s interview. How can production choices persuade?
A celebrity shares political advice with no expertise. What should followers separate?
A local story becomes national outrage overnight. What context may be lost?
A post says “do your own research” but links only one source. What is missing?
A headline quotes one sentence from a long report. What should you check before reacting?
A brand takes a public stance during controversy. What incentives might shape the message?
A video thumbnail makes someone look guilty before evidence appears. What is the framing doing?
A social feed keeps showing you the same opinion. How might that change what feels normal?
A news outlet corrects a story quietly after the damage is done. What should responsible readers do?
Science & Evidence: A headline says people who drink coffee live longer. What would you ask before assuming coffee caused it?
A supplement company cites one small study. What would make the evidence stronger?
A school says test scores improved after a new program. What else could explain the change?
A weather forecast says there is a 70% chance of rain. What does that actually mean for your plans?
A study uses only 20 people. What limits does that create?
A medical trial shows benefits but also side effects. What should patients compare?
A nutrition video says one food causes weight gain. What evidence would you need?
A company tests its own product and reports great results. What bias should you consider?
A lab result changes after a second test. Why can repeating a test matter?
A study finds a link between screen time and anxiety. What would show whether one causes the other?
A toothpaste ad says nine out of ten dentists recommend it. What should you ask about the survey?
A school tries a new reading program and scores rise. What control group question matters?
A fitness study uses only young athletes. Can it apply to older beginners?
A medical headline reports a breakthrough after one early trial. What should patients wait to know?
A sleep app says users feel better after using it. What evidence is missing?
A study finds people who walk more are happier. What else could explain the connection?
A nutrition claim is based on animal studies. What should you know before applying it to people?
A drug reduces symptoms for some patients but not others. What should researchers ask next?
A weather model changes its forecast overnight. Why is updating not the same as being unreliable?
A study has a large sample but weak measurement. Why can size alone be insufficient?
A product claims to be scientifically proven. What does that phrase need to mean?
A survey says teens are more anxious than before. What measurement questions should be asked?
A lab test gives false positives sometimes. What does that mean for one person’s result?
A headline says chocolate improves memory. What study details would matter most?
A researcher has funding from the company being tested. How should that affect scrutiny?
A study shows an average improvement. Why should you ask who did not improve?
A new diet works for your friend. What would make it evidence beyond one story?
A scientist says the evidence is mixed. Why might that be more honest than certainty?
A school bans a chemical after one alarming report. What risk question should be asked?
A medical test is accurate 95% of the time. What else do you need to know to interpret it?
History & Civilization: During the Cuban Missile Crisis, both sides could have escalated. What choices helped avoid disaster?
Kodak invented digital photography but stayed attached to film. What historical pattern does that show?
When Rome weakened, some blamed leaders while others blamed institutions. What evidence would you compare?
The Berlin Airlift resisted pressure without direct war. What can leaders learn from that?
The Challenger disaster followed ignored warnings. What does it teach about institutions under pressure?
The Marshall Plan rebuilt economies after war. Why might helping former enemies be strategic?
Civil Rights marchers stayed disciplined while cameras showed unfair treatment. What made that strategy powerful?
The Industrial Revolution created wealth and harsh working conditions. What tradeoff should history remember?
Apollo 13 succeeded because engineers adapted under pressure. What habits helped them solve the crisis?
After World War I, punishment shaped Europe’s future. What can peace agreements learn from that?
The Roman Republic struggled when personal ambition overpowered institutions. What warning does that offer?
During the American Revolution, colonists risked security for self-government. What tradeoff were they accepting?
The Great Depression changed trust in markets and government. What should leaders learn from crisis responses?
The fall of the Berlin Wall changed Europe quickly. What risks come when old systems collapse?
Toyota’s production system empowered workers to stop the line. What does that show about institutional trust?
The Tylenol recall protected customers at major cost. What did Johnson & Johnson protect besides sales?
During the Cold War, deterrence depended on restraint. What makes restraint hard when fear is high?
The printing press spread knowledge and conflict. What does that reveal about new communication tools?
Ancient Athens valued citizen debate but excluded many people. What should we admire and question?
The Civil Rights Movement used legal strategy and public protest. Why did combining methods matter?
The Roman Empire built roads that connected trade and armies. How can infrastructure strengthen and expose a civilization?
After 9/11, leaders faced pressure to act quickly. What risks come with decisions made in fear?
The Dust Bowl showed how farming practices damaged land. What does that teach about ignoring long-term systems?
Prohibition tried to solve a social problem through law. What unintended consequences followed?
The Space Race pushed innovation through national competition. What can competition create and distort?
World War II codebreakers changed outcomes quietly. What does that show about invisible advantages?
The Magna Carta limited royal power. Why do institutions matter when leaders change?
The Renaissance revived old ideas in new settings. What does that show about learning from the past?
The Salem witch trials spread through fear and accusation. What warning does that offer communities?
The Montgomery Bus Boycott lasted over a year. What does that show about discipline and pressure?
Technology & AI: A school lets students use AI for homework but not tests. What problem does that solve, and what problem could it create?
A company uses AI to screen job applicants. What risks should leaders consider before relying on it?
A deepfake video of a principal spreads online. What should people verify before reacting?
A social media app recommends videos based on watch time. What behavior might that encourage?
A family installs smart cameras inside the home. What privacy tradeoff should they discuss?
A driverless car must choose between two bad outcomes. Who should decide the rules?
A company automates customer service to cut costs. What human problem might get worse?
A teenager uses ChatGPT to write an essay. What learning might they gain or lose?
A hospital uses an algorithm to prioritize patients. What should be checked for fairness?
A business stores customer data to personalize service. What responsibility comes with that information?
A teacher uses AI to grade essays quickly. What might the AI miss?
A company tracks employee keystrokes to measure productivity. What behavior could that create?
A bank uses an algorithm to approve loans. What questions should borrowers and regulators ask?
A student uses AI to study but never checks the answer. What habit could develop?
A news site uses AI images in articles. What should readers be told?
A city installs facial recognition cameras downtown. What safety and privacy tradeoff appears?
A company replaces entry-level workers with automation. What future talent problem might appear?
A social app hides how its feed chooses posts. What power does that give the platform?
A parent uses monitoring software on a teenager’s phone. When does protection become control?
A business uses AI to write apology emails after service failures. What might customers notice?
A school bans AI completely. What learning opportunity might it miss?
A small business uses AI to create ads. What should it check before publishing?
A fake audio clip spreads before an election. What should voters do before believing it?
A company lets AI summarize legal documents. What should a person still review carefully?
A fitness app gives health advice from user data. What risk comes if the advice is wrong?
A chatbot gives confident answers with no source. What should the user ask for?
A platform rewards posts that cause outrage. What design incentive is at work?
A worker uses AI to finish tasks faster but hides it from their boss. What should they consider?
A school uses lockdown software during online tests. What trust issue can that create?
A company adopts new software because competitors do. What question should it ask first?
Creativity & Innovation: A small restaurant is losing lunch customers. Besides lowering prices, what could it test?
LEGO nearly went bankrupt before simplifying its product line. What does that suggest about solving complex problems?
Airbnb began by renting air mattresses during a conference. What does that show about testing small ideas?
Dyson built many prototypes before the vacuum worked. What does persistence add to creativity?
Apple removed buttons from the iPhone keyboard. What risk comes with simplifying a familiar product?
A school cafeteria has long lines. What could it change besides adding workers?
A bookstore loses foot traffic to online shopping. What experience could it create that websites cannot?
A coach has a slower team than opponents. What strategy could turn that constraint into an advantage?
A family has a small backyard but wants a garden. What creative constraint could guide the design?
A company’s meetings waste time. What experiment could reduce meetings without hurting communication?
A teacher’s students are bored by review days. What new format could make practice active?
A mechanic wants customers to trust repair recommendations. What transparent process could help?
A local gym loses members after New Year’s. What habit-based service could it test?
A clothing brand has leftover fabric. What new product could turn waste into value?
A museum wants teenagers to visit. What experience could connect exhibits to their lives?
A city has empty storefronts downtown. What temporary uses could bring people back?
A startup has little ad money. What referral idea could spread the product?
A restaurant gets complaints about waiting. What could customers do while they wait that adds value?
A classroom has limited supplies. How could students still build a strong project?
A church wants more young families involved. What new format could lower the barrier?
A car wash has slow weekdays. What partnership could create demand?
A hotel has unused lobby space. What local service could it host?
A podcast has good content but few listeners. What creative distribution idea could help?
A school club needs fundraising without selling candy. What value could it offer?
A barber shop wants repeat customers. What membership idea could it test?
A small farm wants direct customers. What story or experience could it build around the product?
A nonprofit has many volunteers once a year but not monthly. What system could keep people engaged?
A repair shop wants to teach customers basic maintenance. What simple event could build trust?
A library wants people to see it as modern. What service could surprise them?
A business has too many ideas and no focus. What test could reveal the best one?
Work, Purpose & Ambition: Someone hates their job but has no savings. What should they think through before quitting?
A worker is offered higher pay with less flexibility. What tradeoffs should they evaluate before accepting?
A teenager wants success but avoids hard practice. What is the gap between ambition and action?
A person starts a business while working full-time. What discipline will matter most?
An employee wants a promotion but avoids feedback. What should they change first?
A college student chooses a major only because it sounds impressive. What should they examine?
A worker says they are burned out but never sets boundaries. What conversation might be needed?
A person wants to change careers after one bad week. What should they test before deciding?
An apprentice is bored by basic tasks. What could those tasks be teaching?
A salesperson has talent but refuses to track results. What does ambition need besides confidence?
A manager offers overtime that pays well but strains family life. What should the worker weigh?
A person compares their progress to someone on social media. What might they be missing?
A worker wants meaningful work but ignores pay and stability. What balance should they consider?
A student wants to be an entrepreneur but avoids learning sales. What skill gap matters?
A person stays in a comfortable job that is shrinking. What warning signs should they notice?
A young worker gets criticism from a tough supervisor. How can they separate insult from useful feedback?
A person says they want discipline but keeps changing goals. What would commitment look like?
A parent wants their child to choose a “safe” career. What should both sides discuss?
An employee does extra work but never communicates results. What opportunity might they miss?
A worker is loyal to a company that keeps passing them over. What should they evaluate?
A person starts a side project but quits when it gets boring. What does that reveal about purpose?
A college graduate takes the first job offered out of fear. What should they consider before accepting?
A business owner works constantly but avoids delegation. What ceiling does that create?
A worker wants respect but shows up late. What does reliability communicate?
A person wants a leadership role but avoids responsibility for mistakes. What gap should they close?
A student chooses easy wins instead of hard skills. What future cost could appear?
A person is good at starting things but poor at finishing. What system could help?
A worker changes jobs every time conflict appears. What pattern should they examine?
A person wants more money but refuses to learn new skills. What tradeoff are they choosing?
An ambitious person sacrifices health for achievement. When does drive become self-sabotage?
Identity & Human Behavior: Someone gets defensive every time they receive feedback. What might be happening beneath that reaction?
A person says they value honesty but avoids hard conversations. What does that reveal about self-image?
A teenager acts confident online but anxious in person. What might the performance be protecting?
A friend keeps choosing the same unhealthy relationship pattern. What should they notice?
A parent reacts harshly when a child makes mistakes. What fear might be underneath?
A worker procrastinates on tasks that could improve their future. What emotion might be driving avoidance?
A person apologizes often but never changes the behavior. What pattern should be examined?
A student gives up quickly when they are not naturally good at something. What belief about identity might be limiting them?
A person cannot enjoy success because they expect something bad to happen. What habit of mind is operating?
A friend takes every disagreement as rejection. What might they be confusing?
A person spends money to impress people they barely like. What need might that reveal?
A worker feels angry when others succeed. What comparison trap might be at work?
A teenager follows the group even when uncomfortable. What pressure is shaping identity?
A person says they are “just unlucky” after repeated poor choices. What responsibility question should they ask?
A parent wants control because they fear failure. How can love and fear get mixed?
A person avoids trying because failure would hurt their self-image. What is pride costing them?
A student hides confusion to look smart. What learning opportunity is lost?
A person keeps old grudges alive. What identity can resentment create?
A friend changes personality around different groups. What should they ask about belonging?
A person overreacts to small criticism after a stressful week. What context might explain the reaction?
A worker is praised for being helpful but feels used. What boundary issue might exist?
A person says yes to everyone and then feels resentful. What pattern should they notice?
A teenager wants independence but avoids responsibility. What mismatch appears?
A person trusts strangers online more than people who know them. What need might the internet be filling?
A student cheats once and says it does not represent them. How do choices shape identity over time?
A person uses humor to avoid serious conversations. What might they be protecting?
A friend says they are loyal but disappears during hard times. What does behavior reveal?
A person defines themselves by one failure. What evidence would challenge that identity?
A worker needs constant praise to feel confident. What happens when praise stops?
A person wants change but keeps the same environment. What makes behavior hard to shift?
Literature & Timeless Wisdom: In The Great Gatsby, Gatsby gains wealth but not peace. What warning does that offer about chasing status?
In The Odyssey, Odysseus often survives by patience and cunning. When is restraint stronger than force?
In Aesop’s fable about the boy who cried wolf, trust disappears after repeated lies. What real-life warning does that give?
In To Kill a Mockingbird, Atticus defends an unpopular client. What does courage look like when the crowd disagrees?
In The Prince, Machiavelli warns leaders about power and appearance. What modern leadership risk does that reveal?
In The Art of War, Sun Tzu values winning before fighting. What real-life conflict could be avoided through preparation?
In Shakespeare’s Macbeth, ambition grows into destruction. When does ambition become dangerous?
In Hamlet, overthinking delays action. When can thinking too long become its own mistake?
In The Tortoise and the Hare, slow consistency beats careless talent. Where does that show up in real life?
In The Giving Tree, generosity becomes complicated. When can giving too much become unhealthy?
In A Christmas Carol, Scrooge changes after seeing consequences. Why do people often need a future shock to change?
In The Lord of the Rings, power tempts even good people. What does that teach about character under pressure?
In Animal Farm, language is used to control memory. Where do slogans replace thinking today?
In The Odyssey, the crew ignores warnings and pays for it. Why do groups follow bad impulses?
In Romeo and Juliet, family conflict destroys the young. What does inherited hatred cost?
In The Grapes of Wrath, hardship tests dignity. What helps people stay human under pressure?
In The Little Prince, adults miss what matters by chasing status. What does that warn us about?
In The Intelligent Investor, Graham separates price from value. What life decisions also confuse price with value?
In The Old Man and the Sea, persistence continues after obvious loss. When is endurance noble, and when is it denial?
In Aesop’s ant and grasshopper story, preparation matters before winter. What modern habit does that point to?
In The Crucible, fear turns accusation into power. What warning does that give communities?
In The Great Gatsby, people use parties to hide loneliness. What does attention fail to solve?
In The Odyssey, pride brings trouble after victory. Why can winning make people careless?
In Shakespeare’s King Lear, flattery beats honesty at first. Why do leaders fall for praise?
In The Art of War, knowing yourself matters as much as knowing the opponent. Where does that apply outside war?
In To Kill a Mockingbird, children learn by watching adults. What responsibility does example create?
In The Prince, fear can control people briefly. Why might trust last longer?
In Aesop’s bundle of sticks, unity creates strength. When does a group become weaker by dividing?
In The Alchemist, the journey changes the seeker. Why do goals often teach more than the reward?
In The Odyssey, home matters after adventure. What does that suggest about success without belonging?
